common_name: Bleu de Solaise Leek (Blue of Solaise)

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors in spring; harvest runs from autumn through winter, with flavor and hardiness improving after frost. Bleu de Solaise is a renowned French winter leek from the commune of Solaise in the Rhône valley near Lyon — prized for its extraordinary cold hardiness and the dramatic blue-green to steel-blue coloration the foliage develops in response to frost, one of the most visually striking of all leek varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost date; sow 1/4 inch deep in flats or cell trays at 60–70°F. Bleu de Solaise is a traditional French winter leek from the Rhône valley region near Lyon, classified as a winter-type leek because it is sown in late winter or early spring, transplanted in late spring, and harvested from autumn through the following spring — the main harvesting season runs from November through February in most climates. The most distinctive characteristic of Bleu de Solaise is its dramatic foliage color: the leaves develop an intense steel-blue to blue-violet pigmentation in response to cold temperatures through the anthocyanin response, making each plant increasingly vibrant and beautiful as winter progresses; in hard frosts the blue becomes almost purple-blue. Transplant seedlings in late spring when 6–8 inches tall, planting deeply in a trench or dibbling holes 6 inches deep to blanch the shanks and develop long, tender white stems; the thick shanks (6–8 inches long) have a sweet, mild, sophisticated flavor that intensifies rather than diminishes in cold weather. One of the most cold-tolerant of all leeks, surviving temperatures well below 0°F when established.
sources: French Ministry of Agriculture CTPS Variety Registry

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Autumn Giant is a traditional European leek bred for very large shaft size — producing enormous, thick, long white shanks weighing up to 1–1.5 lbs per plant — and timed for autumn harvest from October through December before the hardest winter weather arrives.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; sow 1/4 inch deep in trays at 65–70°F; transplant when seedlings are pencil-thick, 6–8 inches tall, in late spring. Autumn Giant (sometimes listed as 'Autumn Giant Carentan' in older European catalogs) is a traditional European heirloom leek developed specifically for the production of very large, thick-shafted leeks harvested in autumn — it consistently produces some of the most substantial, heavy-yielding individual leek plants of any variety, with shanks that can exceed 2–3 inches in diameter and 8–10 inches of usable white shank. The name refers to both the size (genuinely giant compared to most leek varieties) and the harvest timing: Autumn Giant is best harvested from September through December, before the coldest winter weather arrives, as it does not have the extreme winter hardiness of specifically bred winter leeks like Bleu de Solaise or Musselburgh. Plant deeply in a trench or use a dibble to create 6-inch planting holes to encourage long, blanched shanks; the rich, mild, sweet flavor is excellent for any leek preparation.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society Vegetable Trial Reports

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Southport Red Globe is a classic American heirloom red storage onion from Southport, Connecticut — where it was the foundation of a thriving 19th-century commercial onion trade — producing medium to large, globe-shaped, deep red-purple bulbs with white, mildly pungent flesh and excellent long storage quality.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost when seedlings are 6–8 inches tall and pencil-width. Southport Red Globe is a long-day heirloom red onion from Southport (now part of Fairfield), Connecticut, where it was the commercial foundation of one of the most important onion-growing regions of 19th-century America — the Connecticut shoreline towns of Southport, Wethersfield, and Granby exported enormous quantities of dried storage onions to markets up and down the Eastern Seaboard and into the Caribbean. The medium to large (3–4 inch diameter), globe-shaped bulbs have deep red-purple skin and white flesh lightly tinged with red at the outer rings; the flavor is moderately pungent when raw, milder when cooked, with a typical red onion character. As a long-day type, Southport Red Globe performs best in northern gardens (zones 3–7) where the long summer days trigger good bulb development; in Gulf Coast gardens (short-day conditions), use short-day red onion varieties instead. Excellent storage quality — properly cured bulbs keep 4–6 months.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant onion sets directly 4–6 weeks before last frost; Stuttgarter Riesen ('Stuttgart Giant') is a traditional German heirloom short-to-medium-day onion prized throughout Europe for its distinctive flat-globe shape, pale yellow-brown skin, mild-sweet flavor, and exceptional keeping quality — the standard European storage onion for much of the 20th century.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost, or plant sets directly in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost at 2–4 inches apart; for seed-grown bulbs, thin to 4–6 inches in rows 12–18 inches apart. Stuttgarter Riesen (literally 'Stuttgart Giant') is a traditional German heirloom onion named for Stuttgart in Baden-Württemberg and widely grown throughout Central Europe as the standard yellow storage onion; it is recognized by its distinctive flattened or slightly disc-shaped globe form (wider than tall), straw-yellow to golden-brown thin papery skin, and crisp white flesh with a mild-sweet flavor that is less pungent than many other yellow storage types. The relatively flat shape and thin skin were historically valued because the flatter bulbs cured and dried quickly without molding, and the thin skin peeled easily — practical advantages that made Stuttgarter both the preferred home-garden and commercial variety. Now widely grown from sets (small bulblets sold by the bag) as well as from seed; set-planted Stuttgarter is often the earliest onion to mature in northern gardens. Exceptional storage quality — properly cured bulbs keep 6–9 months in cool, dry conditions.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Red Wethersfield is one of the oldest documented American onion varieties, originating in Wethersfield, Connecticut, where it was the commercial engine of an extraordinarily productive onion trade with the West Indies and Eastern Seaboard ports that began in the late 1600s and made the town famous throughout Colonial and Federal-era America.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost when seedlings are 6–8 inches tall; direct seed outdoors can also be attempted in early spring when soil is workable. Red Wethersfield (also called 'Large Red Wethersfield') is perhaps the oldest onion variety with documented continuous American cultivation, grown commercially in Wethersfield, Connecticut since the late 17th century; colonial-era records describe the Wethersfield onion trade as one of the most profitable agricultural enterprises of early New England, with flat-bottomed boats (called 'onion sloops') carrying Wethersfield red onions to New York, Boston, and West Indies markets. The medium to large (3–4 inch), flattened-globe bulbs have deep red-purple to dark crimson skin and white-to-pinkish flesh with a moderately pungent flavor that mellows to mild sweetness when cooked. A long-day variety suited to zones 3–7 northern growing conditions where summer day length exceeds 14–16 hours; the thick skin provides excellent storage quality with bulbs keeping 4–6 months when properly cured in a warm, dry location before transferring to cool storage.
